title: References vs Pointers
question: How do references differ from pointers in C++?
answer: A pointer can be null and reassigned; a reference must be initialised and always refers to a single object.
explanation: Prefer references for parameters that must exist; use pointers when null/optional or ownership is involved.
codeExample:
void inc(int& x) { ++x; }
void maybe(int* p) { if (p) ++*p; }
bestPractices: Prefer references or std::optional over raw pointers.
commonMistakes: Dangling references from returning locals.
interviewTips: Discuss const-references for cheap read-only params.

title: RAII
question: What is RAII?
answer: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization — resources (memory, files, locks) are tied to object lifetime; destructors clean up automatically.
explanation: RAII prevents leaks and makes exception safety natural.
bestPractices: Use unique_ptr/shared_ptr instead of raw new/delete.
commonMistakes: Mixing manual delete with smart pointers.
interviewTips: Explain rule of 0/3/5.

title: Smart Pointers
question: Compare unique_ptr, shared_ptr, and weak_ptr.
answer: unique_ptr owns uniquely; shared_ptr shares ownership via ref-counting; weak_ptr observes without extending lifetime, useful for breaking cycles.
explanation: Prefer unique_ptr by default; use shared_ptr only when ownership is genuinely shared.
codeExample:
auto p = std::make_unique<Widget>();
auto s = std::make_shared<Widget>();
bestPractices: Use make_unique/make_shared.
commonMistakes: Creating cycles with shared_ptr causing leaks.
interviewTips: Show weak_ptr breaking a cycle.

title: Move Semantics
question: What are move semantics and rvalue references?
answer: Rvalue references (T&&) allow moving resources from temporaries, avoiding deep copies.
explanation: std::move casts to an rvalue, enabling move constructors/operators.
codeExample:
std::vector<int> a{1,2,3};
auto b = std::move(a); // a is now empty
bestPractices: Make move constructors noexcept.
commonMistakes: Using a moved-from object as if intact.
interviewTips: Explain when copies are elided by RVO.

title: Templates
question: What are templates and how do they enable generic programming?
answer: Templates generate code for specific types at compile time. They power the STL, generic algorithms, and type traits.
explanation: Concepts (C++20) constrain templates and improve error messages.
codeExample:
template<typename T>
T max_of(T a, T b) { return a > b ? a : b; }
bestPractices: Use concepts to constrain generics.
commonMistakes: Instantiation errors from over-generic templates.
interviewTips: Discuss SFINAE briefly.

title: Const Correctness
question: What is const correctness and why does it matter?
answer: Const correctness means marking objects, references, pointers, and member functions const wherever mutation is not intended, so the compiler enforces immutability.
explanation: A const member function promises not to modify observable state; const references let callers pass objects cheaply without allowing changes. It documents intent and catches accidental writes at compile time.
bestPractices: Mark member functions const whenever they do not mutate state.
Pass read-only parameters as const references.
commonMistakes: Casting away const with const_cast to work around design flaws.
Forgetting const on getters.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between a const pointer and a pointer to const.

title: Virtual Functions & vtables
question: How do virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism, and what is a vtable?
answer: A virtual function is resolved at runtime via a per-class virtual table (vtable) of function pointers; each polymorphic object holds a hidden vptr pointing to its class's vtable.
explanation: When you call a virtual function through a base pointer/reference, the vptr is dereferenced to select the correct override. This adds one indirection and prevents inlining. Always give polymorphic base classes a virtual destructor.
bestPractices: Declare a virtual destructor in polymorphic base classes.
Use override to catch signature mismatches.
commonMistakes: Omitting the virtual destructor, causing undefined behavior on delete.
Calling virtual functions from constructors/destructors expecting dynamic dispatch.
interviewTips: Describe the memory cost of a vptr and when to avoid virtuals in hot paths.

title: Rule of 0/3/5
question: Explain the Rule of Zero, Three, and Five.
answer: Rule of Three: if you define a destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment, you likely need all three. Rule of Five adds move constructor and move assignment. Rule of Zero: prefer designing classes so you need none of them.
explanation: These special members manage resource ownership. Defining one usually means non-trivial ownership, so the others must be consistent. The best design uses RAII members (smart pointers, containers) so the compiler-generated versions are correct — that is the Rule of Zero.
bestPractices: Aim for Rule of Zero by using RAII members.
If you declare one special member, consider =default or =delete for the rest.
commonMistakes: Defining a destructor but forgetting the copy/move operations.
Shallow-copying owned pointers leading to double free.
interviewTips: Connect the rule to exception safety and ownership semantics.

title: Copy vs Move Constructors
question: What is the difference between a copy constructor and a move constructor?
answer: A copy constructor duplicates the source object's resources, leaving the source unchanged. A move constructor transfers the source's resources, leaving it in a valid but unspecified (usually empty) state.
explanation: Move construction is selected for rvalues (temporaries or std::move results) and avoids expensive deep copies. Move operations should be marked noexcept so containers can use them during reallocation.
bestPractices: Mark move operations noexcept.
Leave moved-from objects in a valid, destructible state.
commonMistakes: Reading a moved-from object's value.
Non-noexcept move preventing container optimizations.
interviewTips: Explain how RVO/copy elision can skip both constructors.

title: Operator Overloading
question: How and when should you overload operators in C++?
answer: Operator overloading lets user-defined types support built-in syntax like +, ==, or <<. Overload only when it makes the type's semantics clearer and matches conventional meaning.
explanation: Prefer non-member operators for symmetry (especially with implicit conversions), implement compound assignment (+=) then define + in terms of it, and keep behavior intuitive. C++20 lets you default operator== and use the spaceship operator<=> for ordering.
bestPractices: Implement + in terms of +=.
Use non-member friends for symmetric binary operators.
commonMistakes: Overloading operators with surprising semantics.
Returning references to local objects from arithmetic operators.
interviewTips: Mention operator<=> (spaceship) for generating comparisons in C++20.

title: STL Containers & Complexity
question: How do you choose among vector, list, map, and unordered_map by complexity?
answer: vector gives O(1) amortized push_back and O(1) random access with contiguous memory; list gives O(1) splice/insert but O(n) lookup; map is an ordered tree with O(log n) operations; unordered_map is a hash table with average O(1), worst O(n).
explanation: Cache locality often makes vector faster than list even for insertions in the middle for small/medium sizes. Choose ordered map when you need sorted iteration; choose unordered_map for pure key lookups. Measure before optimizing.
codeExample:
std::vector<int> v;      v.push_back(1);                 // O(1) amortized
std::map<std::string,int> m;   m["a"] = 1;             // O(log n)
std::unordered_map<std::string,int> um; um["a"] = 1;   // avg O(1)
bestPractices: Default to vector unless a specific access pattern demands otherwise.
reserve() vectors when the size is known to avoid reallocations.
commonMistakes: Using std::list by default and losing cache performance.
Assuming unordered_map is always O(1) despite worst-case collisions.
interviewTips: Discuss iterator/reference invalidation rules per container.

title: Iterators
question: What are iterators and what iterator categories exist?
answer: Iterators are objects that abstract traversal over a range. Categories are input, output, forward, bidirectional, random-access, and (C++20) contiguous, each supporting progressively more operations.
explanation: Algorithms are written against iterator categories, so the same std::sort works on any random-access range. Knowing categories tells you which algorithms are efficient — std::sort needs random-access, std::list must use its member sort.
bestPractices: Prefer range-based for and algorithms over manual index loops.
Use cbegin/cend when you do not need to modify elements.
commonMistakes: Using an invalidated iterator after container modification.
Comparing iterators from different containers.
interviewTips: Explain why std::list has its own sort member function.

title: Lambdas & Captures
question: How do lambda expressions and their capture modes work?
answer: A lambda is an anonymous function object. Captures bring outer variables into scope: [=] copies by value, [&] captures by reference, or you capture specific variables; [this] captures the enclosing object.
explanation: Value captures are snapshots taken at creation; reference captures alias the original and can dangle if the lambda outlives them. Mutable allows modifying value-captured copies. Generic lambdas use auto parameters, and init-captures (C++14) let you move into a lambda.
codeExample:
int base = 10;
auto add = [base](int x) { return x + base; };     // by value
auto acc = [&base](int x) { base += x; };            // by reference
auto own = [p = std::make_unique<int>(5)]{ return *p; }; // init-capture
bestPractices: Capture only what you need instead of [=] or [&].
Avoid capturing by reference for lambdas stored beyond the current scope.
commonMistakes: Dangling reference captures in stored callbacks.
Capturing this by value when the object may be destroyed.
interviewTips: Show an init-capture that moves a unique_ptr into a lambda.

title: Perfect Forwarding
question: What is perfect forwarding and how do std::forward and forwarding references enable it?
answer: Perfect forwarding passes arguments to another function preserving their value category (lvalue/rvalue) and const-ness, using a forwarding reference (T&&) plus std::forward<T>.
explanation: In a template, T&& is a forwarding reference that binds to both lvalues and rvalues via reference collapsing. std::forward conditionally casts back to an rvalue only when the original was an rvalue, so wrappers like make_unique or emplace forward args without extra copies.
bestPractices: Use std::forward<T> exactly once per forwarded parameter.
Constrain forwarding constructors so they don't hijack copy/move.
commonMistakes: Using std::move instead of std::forward, moving an lvalue unexpectedly.
Forgetting reference collapsing rules.
interviewTips: Explain reference collapsing: T& && -> T&, T&& && -> T&&.

title: constexpr
question: What does constexpr mean and how does it differ from const?
answer: constexpr guarantees a value or function can be evaluated at compile time; const only means an object cannot be modified after initialization (which may still happen at runtime).
explanation: constexpr functions run at compile time when given constant arguments, enabling compile-time computation, array sizes, and template arguments. C++20 adds consteval (must be compile-time) and constinit. This can reduce runtime work and enable stronger static checks.
bestPractices: Mark pure, side-effect-free functions constexpr where useful.
Use static_assert to validate compile-time results.
commonMistakes: Assuming constexpr forces compile-time evaluation in every context.
Confusing const with constexpr.
interviewTips: Mention consteval and constinit added in C++20.

title: Undefined Behavior
question: What is undefined behavior (UB) and why is it dangerous?
answer: Undefined behavior is any operation the C++ standard imposes no requirements on — the compiler may do anything, including crashing, producing wrong results, or appearing to work until it doesn't.
explanation: Common UB sources include out-of-bounds access, signed integer overflow, dereferencing null/dangling pointers, data races, and using uninitialized values. Optimizers assume UB never happens, so code can be silently removed or transformed. Use sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) to catch it.
bestPractices: Enable -fsanitize=address,undefined in test builds.
Prefer bounds-checked access (.at()) during development.
commonMistakes: Relying on observed behavior of UB across compilers.
Assuming signed overflow wraps like unsigned.
interviewTips: Give a concrete example where the optimizer exploits UB.

title: Exceptions & noexcept
question: How does exception handling work and what does noexcept guarantee?
answer: Exceptions transfer control from a throw site up the stack to a matching catch, unwinding and destroying local objects along the way. noexcept declares a function will not throw; if it does, std::terminate is called.
explanation: RAII makes exception safety natural because destructors release resources during unwinding. noexcept enables optimizations — notably, containers use move (instead of copy) during reallocation only if the move constructor is noexcept. Never let destructors throw.
bestPractices: Mark move constructors and swap as noexcept.
Catch exceptions by const reference.
commonMistakes: Throwing from a destructor.
Marking a function noexcept then letting it throw.
interviewTips: Explain the basic, strong, and nothrow exception guarantees.

title: Multiple Inheritance & Diamond
question: What is the diamond problem and how does virtual inheritance solve it?
answer: The diamond problem arises when a class inherits from two classes that share a common base, creating two copies of the base subobject. Virtual inheritance makes them share a single base instance.
explanation: Declaring the intermediate classes' inheritance as virtual ensures the most-derived class constructs one shared base. This adds complexity (the most-derived class initializes the virtual base) and some overhead, so prefer composition or interface-only (pure abstract) bases where possible.
bestPractices: Prefer interface (pure abstract) classes for multiple inheritance.
Use virtual inheritance only when a shared base is truly required.
commonMistakes: Ambiguous member access without virtual inheritance.
Forgetting the most-derived class must initialize the virtual base.
interviewTips: Contrast multiple inheritance with composition and mixins.

title: std::optional, variant, any
question: When would you use std::optional, std::variant, and std::any?
answer: std::optional<T> represents a value that may be absent; std::variant<Ts...> is a type-safe tagged union holding exactly one of several types; std::any holds a value of any type with runtime type erasure.
explanation: optional replaces sentinel values and nullable returns cleanly. variant models closed sets of alternatives visited with std::visit. any is the most flexible but least type-safe and should be rare. All three (C++17) avoid manual unions and null pointers.
bestPractices: Return std::optional instead of magic sentinel values.
Use std::visit with variant for exhaustive handling.
commonMistakes: Dereferencing an empty optional.
Accessing the wrong alternative of a variant (throws std::bad_variant_access).
interviewTips: Explain why variant is preferable to a hand-written tagged union.

title: Concurrency Basics
question: How do std::thread, std::mutex, and std::atomic help write concurrent code?
answer: std::thread launches work on another thread; std::mutex (with lock_guard/unique_lock) provides mutual exclusion to protect shared data; std::atomic offers lock-free operations on single values with defined memory ordering.
explanation: Unsynchronized access to shared mutable data is a data race — undefined behavior. Use RAII lock guards to avoid forgetting to unlock, prefer std::atomic for simple counters/flags, and always join or detach threads. Higher-level std::async and futures simplify result passing.
bestPractices: Protect shared data with RAII lock guards.
Prefer std::atomic for simple flags and counters.
commonMistakes: Data races from unsynchronized shared access.
Forgetting to join/detach a thread before it is destroyed.
interviewTips: Discuss deadlock avoidance via consistent lock ordering.
